metadata.description: Event-driven password spray detection Logic App. Polls Entra ID Protection every N minutes for passwordSpray risk detections and immediately alerts the SOC team with affected accounts, source IPs, and investigation context. Uses System-Assigned Managed Identity — no stored credentials or OAuth connectors.
metadata.author: Dr Muataz Awad

metadata.description: Azure region for the Logic App.
UPN of the mailbox to send alerts FROM (e.g. security-alerts@company.com). Must be a valid mailbox in your tenant.
Email address to send alerts TO. Can be a distribution list or individual mailbox.
How often (minutes) to poll Entra ID Protection for new password spray detections. Default: 30 minutes.

Format_Detection_Row.inputs:
@{coalesce(items('For_Each_Detection')?['userDisplayName'], 'Unknown')}
@{coalesce(items('For_Each_Detection')?['userPrincipalName'], '-')}
@{coalesce(items('For_Each_Detection')?['ipAddress'], 'Unknown')}
@{coalesce(items('For_Each_Detection')?['location']?['city'], '')} @{coalesce(items('For_Each_Detection')?['location']?['countryOrRegion'], '-')}
@{toUpper(coalesce(items('For_Each_Detection')?['riskLevel'], 'NONE'))}@{formatDateTime(items('For_Each_Detection')?['detectedDateTime'], 'yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm')} UTCUser Profile  Sign-in Logs

Compose_Email_Body.inputs:
⚠ Password Spray Attack Detected

@{length(body('Parse_PasswordSpray_Detections')?['value'])} detection(s) in the last @{parameters('pollingIntervalMinutes')} minutes • @{formatDateTime(utcNow(), 'dddd, MMMM dd yyyy HH:mm')} UTC

Microsoft Entra ID Protection detected password spray activity targeting accounts in your tenant. Review the affected accounts immediately and initiate containment steps.

@{join(variables('SprayReport'), '')}
AccountSource / LocationRiskDetected AtActions
⚡ Quick Actions — Do these first
  1. Click Sign-in Logs for each user above → filter Status = Success near the detection time. Any success after a burst of failures = compromised account → click User Profile → Revoke sessions immediately.
  2. Block source IP in Entra: Security → Conditional Access → Named Locations → add as blocked IP range. Also block at firewall for instant effect before CA policy propagates.
  3. For each compromised account check: inbox rules (Outlook Settings), new OAuth consents (Entra → Enterprise Apps), new MFA methods (Entra → Users → Authentication methods). Anything added after the detection time is an attacker backdoor.
▼   Detailed step-by-step response guide below   ▼

🔎 Analyst Response Guide — Password Spray
Phase 1 — Immediate  ⏱ 0–15 min
Step 1 — Verify the spray is real
Why: Confirm before taking disruptive action. A spray is defined by many accounts targeted from the same source IP in a short window — not a single failed login.
  • Check the Source IP column above — one IP hitting multiple accounts is the key spray indicator.
  • Check IP reputation at VirusTotal or AbuseIPDB — known Tor/VPN exit nodes confirm a spray campaign.
  • Open Entra Risk Detections to check whether additional accounts beyond this alert were also targeted.
Step 2 — Revoke sessions on confirmed compromises
Why: An account that had a successful sign-in after failed attempts is likely compromised. The attacker has an active session right now and must be cut off immediately.
  • Click Sign-in Logs for each user above → filter by Status = Success near the detection timestamp.
  • For any confirmed post-spray successful login: click User Profile → Revoke sessions button. This kills all refresh tokens within 1 minute.
  • Reset the account password before the user re-authenticates to prevent re-entry with the compromised credential.
  • Sign-in log scoping query: In Entra sign-in logs filter userId = [affected user], Status = Success, time range = last 24 hours. A successful login from a different IP immediately after a burst of failures is the definitive compromise indicator — this is how you distinguish a breached account from one that was merely targeted.
Phase 2 — Containment  ⏱ 15–60 min
Step 3 — Block the attacking IP(s)
Why: Without blocking the source, the attacker rotates target accounts and continues spraying. The same IP is often used for hours or days across multiple tenants.
  • Entra ID → Security → Conditional Access → Named Locations → + IP ranges → add source IP as a blocked location.
  • Also block at the network/firewall layer for immediate effect — CA policy propagation can take a few minutes.
  • If the IP belongs to a VPN/proxy ASN (check AbuseIPDB), consider blocking the full ASN range in CA.
  • Block by user agent: In Entra sign-in logs, filter for the spray source IP and note the User-Agent string — automated spray tools use distinctive values (e.g. python-requests/2.x, Go-http-client/1.1). Add the user agent as a filter condition in Conditional Access or block it at your WAF/network perimeter. This neutralises the same spray tool even when the attacker rotates IPs.
Step 4 — Enforce MFA on all targeted accounts
Why: Even if the password was not successfully guessed, the targeted accounts remain vulnerable to a second spray or credential stuffing from leaked databases.
  • Confirm all targeted accounts are covered by a CA policy requiring MFA at sign-in.
  • Prefer phishing-resistant MFA (FIDO2 key, Windows Hello, Certificate-Based Auth) over SMS/voice, which can be bypassed with SIM-swapping or real-time phishing proxies.
Phase 3 — Post-Compromise Investigation  ⏱ 1–4 hrs
Step 5 — Hunt for attacker activity on breached accounts
Why: Once inside, attackers set up persistence before any alert fires. They may have already exfiltrated data, created forwarding rules, or consented to a malicious OAuth app.
  • Inbox rules: Outlook → Settings → Rules — look for rules that forward, redirect, or delete emails. This is the most common data exfiltration and alert-hiding technique.
  • OAuth consents: Entra → Enterprise Applications → filter by user — any app consented to after the detection time is suspicious and should be revoked.
  • New registered devices: Entra → Users → [user] → Devices — an attacker-registered device can be used to obtain a PRT and maintain persistent access even after password reset.
  • MFA method changes: Entra → Users → [user] → Authentication methods — any new method added after the detection timestamp is an attacker backdoor and must be removed.
Step 6 — Harden against recurrence
Why: Password sprays succeed because many environments lack MFA, use weak lockout policies, or allow commonly sprayed passwords. Closing these gaps eliminates most spray risk.
  • Entra → Security → Authentication methods → Password protection — enable Smart Lockout (threshold: 5 failures, duration: 60 sec).
  • Deploy Microsoft Entra Password Protection to block commonly sprayed weak passwords (Season+Year, CompanyName123, etc.).
  • Enforce MFA via Conditional Access for all users — not just privileged accounts. Most sprays target regular user accounts as an initial foothold.

Suggested Security Copilot Investigation:
Investigate password spray attack from IP [@{coalesce(body('Parse_PasswordSpray_Detections')?['value']?[0]?['ipAddress'], 'detected IP')}] — identify all targeted accounts, confirm which were compromised, review post-compromise activity, and provide containment and hardening steps.

Entra Risk Detections  |  Risky Users  |  Security Copilot

Generated by Password Spray Auto-Alert Logic App • Dr Muataz Awad

message.subject: [CRITICAL] Password Spray Detected — @{length(body('Parse_PasswordSpray_Detections')?['value'])} account(s) targeted — @{formatDateTime(utcNow(), 'yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm')} UTC
